course_info.title: Psychology Capstone Project
course_info.meeting_schedule: Thursday - 3 hours per week (seminar) plus independent research

class_schedule.topic: Capstone Project Launch: From Proposal to Execution
learning_objectives: Refine research proposal from PSY410 into an executable project plan
Establish project timeline with milestones and deliverables
Identify faculty advisor and secure IRB approval pathway
readings: APA Publication Manual, 7th Ed., Ch. 1-2; university IRB application guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone project is the culmination of your undergraduate psychology education. You will conduct an original empirical study -- collecting real data, analyzing it rigorously, and presenting findings in a professional format.
key_concepts: Translating a research proposal into actionable steps
IRB application preparation: informed consent templates, risk assessment
Project management: Gantt chart, milestone tracking, contingency planning
Faculty advisor roles and expectations
Data management plan: collection, storage, security, and sharing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: create a detailed Gantt chart for the semester with weekly milestones

review.value: Must include: study purpose, participant population, recruitment methods, procedures, risks and benefits, informed consent process, data security plan
Key milestones: IRB approval (Week 3), data collection start (Week 4), data collection complete (Week 9), analysis complete (Week 11), final paper (Week 14)

class_schedule.topic: IRB Preparation and Ethical Data Collection
learning_objectives: Complete an IRB application for the capstone project
Develop informed consent documents appropriate for the study population
Plan ethical data collection procedures with attention to participant welfare

lecture_notes.introduction: Before any data can be collected, IRB approval must be obtained. This session ensures your application is complete, ethical, and likely to be approved on first submission.
key_concepts: Elements of the IRB application: purpose, methods, participants, risks, benefits, consent
Risk categorization: minimal risk vs. greater than minimal risk
Informed consent document construction: readability, completeness, voluntariness
Special considerations: deception, vulnerable populations, online data collection
Data security: de-identification, encryption, secure storage
lecture_notes.demonstration: Peer review of draft IRB applications: identify gaps and potential concerns

review.value: The probability and magnitude of harm are not greater than those ordinarily encountered in daily life; most undergraduate projects qualify for expedited review
Remove all 18 HIPAA identifiers; assign participant codes; store the linking key separately from data in a secure location

class_schedule.topic: Data Collection Methods and Quality Control
learning_objectives: Develop quality control checks for data integrity

lecture_notes.introduction: Data quality determines the value of your research. Careful attention to standardized procedures, participant experience, and data integrity is essential.
key_concepts: Standardization: following the protocol exactly for every participant
Quality control: range checks, attention checks, completion rates
Handling missing data: prevention strategies during collection
Participant management: scheduling, no-shows, compensation
Pilot testing: identifying problems before full data collection begins
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a pilot run of data collection procedures and debrief on problems encountered

review.value: Items embedded in surveys to verify participants are reading and responding thoughtfully (e.g., 'Please select Strongly Agree for this item')
Running the full procedure with 3-5 participants to identify problems with instructions, timing, measures, or technology before full data collection

learning_objectives: Clean and prepare raw data for analysis
Interpret results in relation to hypotheses and prior research
readings: SPSS or R analysis tutorials specific to each project's design

lecture_notes.introduction: With data in hand, the analysis phase begins. Following your preregistered analysis plan ensures that results are credible and interpretable.
key_concepts: Data cleaning: identifying and handling outliers, checking assumptions
Following the preregistered analysis plan: primary then secondary analyses
Assumption checking: normality, homogeneity of variance, linearity
Effect size calculation and confidence intervals
Dealing with unexpected results: exploratory analyses must be labeled as such
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live data analysis workshop: students bring datasets and work through analysis with instructor support
setup_requirements: SPSS or R with project datasets

review.value: Analyses not in the preregistration are exploratory and must be labeled as such in the paper; they generate hypotheses for future research, not conclusions
When assumptions are violated, use the contingency plan from your preregistration: non-parametric alternatives, robust estimators, or bootstrap methods

class_schedule.topic: Writing the Research Paper and Preparing the Presentation
learning_objectives: Write results and discussion sections following APA format
Prepare a professional conference-style oral presentation
Create a research poster meeting APA poster guidelines

lecture_notes.introduction: The final phase transforms your research into communicable knowledge. Clear, honest, and professional scientific communication is the last step in the research process.
key_concepts: Results section: statistical findings, tables, and figures in APA format
Discussion section: interpretation, limitations, implications, future directions
Conference presentation skills: structure, visual design, time management
Research poster design: hierarchy, readability, visual flow
Responding to questions: defending methodology and acknowledging limitations gracefully
lecture_notes.demonstration: Presentation practice: 5-minute lightning talks with peer feedback on clarity and engagement

review.value: Brief summary of findings, interpretation in context of hypotheses and prior research, limitations (honest and specific), theoretical and practical implications, specific future directions
Clear visual hierarchy, readable from 4 feet away (24pt minimum), minimal text, prominent figures and tables, logical flow from left to right and top to bottom

major_assignments.title: Original Empirical Research Paper
major_assignments.description: Conduct an original empirical study and write a complete APA-format research paper. The project involves all phases of the research process: IRB approval, data collection, analysis, and professional write-up. This is the capstone demonstration of undergraduate research competence.
objectives: Execute an original empirical research study from start to finish
Collect and analyze real data using appropriate methods
Write a publication-quality research paper in APA format
Present findings in a professional conference-style format
deliverables: Raw data file and analysis output
Research poster or conference presentation slides

criteria: Adequate sample size with power justification
Appropriate reporting of statistics, effect sizes, and confidence intervals
Complete, rigorous empirical study from IRB to final paper
Professional APA-format paper with honest reporting of results

capstone_paper.description: Complete APA research paper reporting original empirical study

research_presentation.description: Conference-style oral presentation or poster session

milestone_completion.description: On-time completion of IRB, data collection, and analysis milestones

seminar_participation.description: Active engagement in peer workshops and feedback sessions

advisor_evaluation.description: Faculty advisor assessment of independence, initiative, and professionalism

course_policies.attendance: Attendance at all seminar sessions is mandatory. Students must also maintain regular meetings with their faculty advisor.
course_policies.late_submissions: Milestone deadlines are firm. Late data collection completion may compromise the ability to complete the project. Final paper is penalized 5% per day late.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer feedback is a core component. Research projects must be independently conducted and written.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Data fabrication or falsification will result in course failure and referral to the academic integrity board. All data must be genuinely collected.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to data collection tools (Qualtrics, PsychoPy, or equivalent), SPSS or R, OSF for preregistration, and poster/presentation software.
